Snipara's tech stack:

- Frontend: Next.js 14, Tailwind, DaisyUI, Tremor  
- Backend: Next.js API Routes, tRPC  
- MCP Server: Python FastAPI  
- Database: PostgreSQL + Prisma  
- Auth: NextAuth.js  
- Billing: Stripe  
- Cache / Rate limiting: Redis  
- Deployment: Vercel (web) and Railway (MCP)

Snipara’s tech stack:

- Frontend: Next.js 14 (App Router), Tailwind CSS, DaisyUI, Tremor  
- Backend (web): Next.js API Routes + tRPC  
- MCP server: Python FastAPI  
- Database: PostgreSQL (Neon) with Prisma (shared schema) and pgvector for vector search  
- Auth: NextAuth.js (GitHub, Google, Email)  
- Billing: Stripe  
- Cache / Rate limiting: Redis  
- Email: Resend or SMTP (config via env vars)  
- Deployment: Vercel for web, Railway for the FastAPI MCP server

Snipara’s tech stack:

- Frontend: Next.js 14 (App Router), Tailwind CSS, DaisyUI, Tremor  
- Backend (web): Next.js API Routes, tRPC  
- MCP server: Python FastAPI  
- Database: PostgreSQL + Prisma (with pgvector for vector search)

- Billing: Stripe  
- Cache / Rate limiting: Redis  
- Deployment: Vercel (web) and Railway (MCP server)

Snipara’s core value proposition is context optimization as a service: it dramatically reduces large documentation into a small, highly relevant context (≈90% reduction — e.g., from ~500K tokens to ~5K), so clients can pass that optimized context to their own LLM (LLM‑agnostic, no vendor lock‑in or LLM inference costs). This enables efficient, cost‑effective LLM usage with high margins.

Snipara’s core value proposition is Context Optimization as a Service: dramatically reduce large documentation corpora into a small, highly relevant context (≈90% reduction — e.g., from ~500K tokens to ~5K tokens), delivered in a token-budget-aware way so clients can use their own LLM (LLM‑agnostic, no vendor lock‑in) while avoiding LLM inference costs — a fast, cost‑efficient way to make LLMs useful on large docs.

Snipara’s core value proposition is Context Optimization as a Service: aggressively compressing large documentation into a small, highly relevant context that clients feed to their own LLMs. Key points from the docs:

- ~90% context reduction (e.g., from ~500K tokens to ~5K tokens of highly relevant content)  
- LLM-agnostic — clients use their own model (no vendor lock-in)  
- No LLM inference costs passed through (pure compute service → high margins)

Short answer
- Snipara exposes 39 MCP tools in total (25 “context” tools + 14 “agent” tools).

What’s named in the provided docs
- Primary / core: rlm_context_query
- Memory tools: rlm_remember, rlm_recall, rlm_memories, rlm_forget
- Shared-context / templates: rlm_shared_context, rlm_list_templates, rlm_get_template, rlm_list_collections, rlm_upload_shared_document
- MCP server (REPL) tools: execute_python, get_repl_context, set_repl_context, clear_repl_context
- Partial tools (have client methods but no command): rlm_stats, rlm_read, rlm_list_collections, rlm_upload_shared_document, rlm_settings
- Missing (no client method): rlm_request_access

Other categories mentioned (names not fully listed in the excerpt)
- Search & retrieval tools
- Context management tools
- Multi‑agent / swarm tools (10 tools for creation/joining, locking, shared state, queues)
- Additional context tools (total 25) and additional agent tools (total 14) make up the full 39.

If you want the complete, named list, see the full MCP tools reference (docs/mcp-tools) — the provided excerpt lists many but not every tool name.

Snipara Context Plans:

- FREE — $0 / mo — 100 queries/month  
- PRO — $19 / mo — 5,000 queries/month  
- TEAM — $49 / mo — 20,000 queries/month  
- ENTERPRISE — $499 / mo — Unlimited queries/month

(Also, the docs note a self‑hosted strategy with FREE/Trial/Licensed tiers (license keys ~$0–$499/mo) and optional Enterprise support at ~$2K–$7.5K/mo.)

Not found explicitly as a single labeled section in the provided documentation, but the repo/docs describe a de facto three‑component architecture. Summarized from the provided material:

1) Client (snipara-mcp)
- Local/client library and CLI users install (pip or uvx).
- Location in monorepo: apps/mcp-server/snipara-mcp/.
- Communicates with the backend over HTTP REST (examples show POSTs to https://api.snipara.com/mcp/<PROJECT_SLUG> and health checks at https://api.snipara.com/health).

2) MCP Server / Backend API (snipara-fastapi)
- FastAPI service that implements authentication, document storage, and the context‑optimization engine.
- Source in apps/mcp-server/src/ (and mirrored to the separate repo github.com/alopez3006/snipara-fastapi).
- Deployed on Railway (domain: api.snipara.com). Key files: src/server.py, src/auth.py, src/rlm_engine.py, src/mcp_transport.py, etc.

3) Web App / Front end (Vercel)
- The public web application deployed on Vercel; connects to the backend and uses the same codebase (monorepo).
- Deployment notes: build command pnpm build, output apps/web/.next, root ., and environment variables must be set.

How they interact (high level)
- Clients (snipara-mcp or other MCP‑compatible clients) call MCP endpoints on the FastAPI backend (api.snipara.com) to run context optimization and agent features.
- The web app uses the backend APIs for UI functionality.
- Development/deployment requires coordinating changes across repos (dual‑repo sync for apps/mcp-server/src/ and using Railway CLI for reliable deployments).

Not found as an explicit "three-component architecture" phrase in the provided docs, but the repository and deployment docs make the system components and their roles clear. Summarizing those into three main components:

1. Client SDKs / Integrations (snipara-mcp)
- The Python client published on PyPI (snipara-mcp) that users install (pip or uvx).
- Communicates with the backend via HTTP REST (or can use stdio transports per .mcp.json).
- Used by tools/clients to query the MCP (e.g., rlm_context_query, rlm_ask, etc.).

2. MCP Backend Server (snipara-fastapi)
- The FastAPI-based MCP server (repo: snipara-fastapi / code in apps/mcp-server/src/) deployed on Railway (api.snipara.com).
- Implements authentication, the context‑optimization engine, the MCP HTTP transport, and the 39 tools exposed via stdio/HTTP.
- Depends on PostgreSQL and Redis services for storage/state per the Railway deployment instructions.

3. Web / Frontend + Deployment & Packaging
- The web app deployed to Vercel (monorepo -> apps/web), providing the user-facing UI and other front-end functionality.
- Also includes the packaging/deployment workflow: code in the monorepo must be synced/pushed to two repos (RLMSaas → Vercel + PyPI, and snipara-fastapi → Railway). This component covers distribution (PyPI) and deployment flows.

How they interact (from the docs)
- Clients use snipara-mcp to call the MCP backend (HTTP REST to api.snipara.com/mcp/... or stdio).
- The FastAPI MCP server handles auth, document/storage, and context optimization, backed by Postgres/Redis.
- The web app and CLI/tools use the same backend APIs; deployment and releases are coordinated across the two repositories and hosting platforms.

The docs don’t use the exact phrase “three‑component architecture,” but the project is clearly organized into three cooperating parts. Summarized from the provided documentation:

1. Client (snipara-mcp)
- The user-facing client library distributed on PyPI (`snipara-mcp`).
- Installed by users and tools; communicates with the backend over HTTP REST.
- Primary way to call Snipara MCP tools (e.g., rlm_context_query, rlm_ask, rlm_search).

2. Backend API / MCP Server (snipara-fastapi)
- The FastAPI MCP server implementation (source in apps/mcp-server/src/ and mirrored to the separate repo `snipara-fastapi`).
- Implements authentication, context-optimization engine, and the MCP HTTP transport (files: server.py, auth.py, rlm_engine.py, mcp_transport.py, etc.).
- Exposes the MCP HTTP endpoints (e.g., https://api.snipara.com/mcp/...) consumed by the client.

3. Deployment / Web & Infrastructure
- Web frontend deployed on Vercel (web app) and the MCP API deployed on Railway (api.snipara.com).
- Railway project hosts the FastAPI service and backing services like PostgreSQL and Redis.
- Deployment workflow includes dual-repo sync (monorepo → RLMSaas and `snipara-fastapi`) and recommended Railway CLI usage (`railway up`) for reliable API deployments.

Together these three components provide the full system: clients call the MCP REST API, the FastAPI backend runs the context-optimization engine and tools, and Vercel/Railway + DB/Redis provide hosting and persistence.

High level summary
- Snipara treats token budgets as a first-class constraint: it measures tokens with tiktoken (cl100k_base) and selects/document‑slices to fit a strict max_tokens budget rather than returning whole documents by default.
- The primary entrypoint is the rlm_context_query tool (default max_tokens=4000) which returns an optimized set of sections plus metadata (file, line ranges, relevance scores, token counts, truncated flags, session_context, suggestions).

Key steps in the budgeting process
1. Search & candidate collection
   - rlm_context_query supports keyword, semantic, or hybrid search to produce candidate sections.

2. Token counting
   - Each candidate’s token count is computed with tiktoken using the cl100k_base encoder.

3. Ranking
   - Candidates are ranked by relevance score (higher first).

4. Greedy selection within the token budget
   - The engine adds highest-relevance sections until adding the next full section would exceed max_tokens.
   - If a partial section can fit, smart_truncate is used to trim the section to the remaining tokens and the section is marked truncated.

Smart truncation behavior
- smart_truncate trims to fit the remaining token budget and tries to cut on sentence boundaries (., !, ?) so content isn’t cut mid-sentence.
- A "..." marker is appended and the section metadata includes truncated: true.
- The response-level truncated flag is set if the overall budget is filled/truncated.

Budget allocation strategies / category allocation
- There are built‑in allocation patterns that prioritize categories. Example allocations shown:
  - CATEGORY_BUDGETS example: MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10%.
  - DEFAULT_ALLOCATION example (shared/project mix): project 40%, MANDATORY 25%, BEST_PRACTICES 20%, GUIDELINES 10%, REFERENCE 5%.
- Allocation logic: project documents are allocated first, then shared docs by category; within each allocation bucket documents are sorted by relevance and added until that category budget is exhausted.
- Hard limits are enforced (examples in code: MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION = 100_000, limits on collections and docs).

Session context and system injection
- Session context can be included in the result and (implementation note) may be allowed up to ~20% of the budget.
- System instructions can be auto-injected into the context where applicable.

Output & metadata
- rlm_context_query returns structured metadata per section:
  - title, content, file path, line ranges, relevance_score, token_count, and truncated if applicable.
- Response fields include sections[], total_tokens, truncated (boolean), session_context, suggestions.
- Usage/search telemetry is also returned (search_time_ms, sections_scanned, cache_hit).

Other relevant behaviors
- Multi-query mode: budgets can be split across multiple queries; each sub-query runs its own context selection with a per-query max_tokens.
- Token efficiency metrics (compression_ratio, token_reduction_pct, cost_savings_pct) are available to measure optimization results.
- Token budgeting is decoupled from LLM calls — the engine optimizes what to send to the model to control token usage and cost.

If you want, I can show a short example of an rlm_context_query request/response or walk through how a particular document set would be allocated under the DEFAULT_ALLOCATION.

Short answer
- Snipara enforces a token budget by counting tokens (with tiktoken / cl100k_base) for candidate context sections, then greedily selecting the highest-relevance sections until the max_tokens budget is reached. Partial sections are smart-truncated at sentence boundaries and flagged as truncated.

How it works (details)
- Token counting
  - Uses tiktoken (cl100k_base) to get accurate token counts for sections and for budgeting. (pyproject adds tiktoken>=0.5.0)
- Primary entrypoint
  - Tool: rlm_context_query
  - Key params: query, max_tokens (default 4000), search_mode (keyword | semantic | hybrid), include_metadata
  - Returns: sections (title, content, file, line ranges, relevance_score, token_count), total_tokens, truncated flag, session_context, suggestions, etc.
- Selection algorithm
  - Candidates are produced by keyword/semantic/hybrid search and ranked by relevance.
  - Greedy selection: iterate highest-relevance first, add whole sections while total_tokens + section_tokens <= max_tokens.
  - If a section would exceed the remaining budget, a partial section is included by calling smart_truncate to fit the remaining tokens and that section is marked truncated.
- Smart truncation
  - Truncates to fit remaining tokens.
  - Cuts at sentence boundaries (. ! ?), appends "..." and sets truncated metadata for the section.
- Metadata & session context
  - Metadata returned per section: file path, line ranges, relevance score, token count, truncated flag.
  - Session context may be included (implementation notes mention session context inclusion up to ~20% of the budget).
- Budget allocation across categories (project/shared)
  - There is an allocation layer that can split a total budget among project documents and shared documents/categories.
  - Example default allocation from the web service code: project 0.40, MANDATORY 0.25, BEST_PRACTICES 0.20, GUIDELINES 0.10, REFERENCE 0.05.
  - There are also category-priority and category-budget examples in other documentation snippets (used to influence selection order).
- Hard limits and enforcement
  - The system defines limits (e.g., MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION) and will truncate/drop documents to meet those limits.
- Multi-query & decomposition support
  - Multi-query flow distributes budgets per subquery and aggregates results.
  - Decompose tool estimates tokens for sub-queries without returning content.
- Cost / efficiency tracking
  - Snipara exposes token_budget options (e.g., CompletionOptions.token_budget) and token-efficiency metrics (compression_ratio, token_reduction_pct, cost_savings_pct) to measure savings.

The net effect
- Snipara optimizes what context to include in an LLM request by measuring real token cost, prioritizing high-relevance sections, allocating budget across categories/sources, and smartly truncating when needed so the returned context fits the requested token budget while preserving metadata and traceability.

Summary — how Snipara's token budgeting works

- Purpose
  - Produce an optimized context payload for a query that fits inside a token budget so downstream LLM calls stay within cost/size limits.

- Where it runs
  - Primary implementation: the rlm_context_query tool in apps/mcp-server/src/rlm_engine.py. It’s used by MCP server APIs and recursive/multi‑query helpers.

- Token counting
  - Uses tiktoken (cl100k_base encoding, tiktoken>=0.5.0) to count tokens for each candidate section and the session/system context.

- Search & ranking
  - Search modes: keyword, semantic, or hybrid (fusion of both).
  - Results are ranked by relevance (relevance_score) before selection.

- Selection algorithm (greedy token budgeting)
  - For a given max_tokens (default example: 4000), each candidate section’s token_count is computed.
  - Sections are selected greedily in descending relevance order.
  - Stop when adding the next full section would exceed the token budget.
  - If part of a section can fit, smart_truncate is applied to include a sentence-trimmed partial section and mark it truncated.

- Smart truncation
  - Truncates content to fit remaining tokens.
  - Cuts at sentence boundaries (., !, ?), appends "..." and flags the section as truncated in metadata.

- Session & system context
  - Session context (previous context) may be included; docs note session context inclusion up to ~20% of the budget.
  - System instructions can be auto-injected as needed when computing the budget.

- Metadata returned
  - Each selected section includes: title, content, file path, line ranges, relevance_score, token_count, and a truncated flag if applicable.
  - The overall response includes total_tokens used, truncated (boolean if budget filled), session_context, and suggestions (related docs).

- Category & project-level allocations
  - Token budgets can be split by category/source. Examples in the docs:
    - CATEGORY_BUDGETS example: MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10%.
    - Web DEFAULT_ALLOCATION example: project 40%, MANDATORY 25%, BEST_PRACTICES 20%, GUIDELINES 10%, REFERENCE 5%.
  - The web-side allocation logic groups docs, computes per-category budgets (totalBudget * allocation), and then fills each bucket by relevance until that bucket is exhausted.

- Allocation mechanics (web code)
  - allocateWithinBudget sorts docs by relevance and includes docs until the category/project budget is exceeded; others are marked unallocated.
  - Hard limits are enforced (examples: MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION = 100_000, limits on collections/docs).

- Multi-query & decomposition support
  - Multi-query handler splits an overall budget among multiple queries and calls rlm_context_query per subquery.
  - A decompose tool can split complex queries into subqueries and estimate token needs before fetching content.

- Metrics & efficiency
  - Token-efficiency metrics are available (compression_ratio, token_reduction_pct, cost_savings_pct) and are computed from token counts (no LLM required).
  - Cost tracking interfaces accept explicit token_budget and cost_budget values for higher-level completions.

- Response shape (example)
  - The rlm_context_query returns a JSON result with sections (with metadata), total_tokens, truncated flag, session_context, and suggestions, plus usage info like search_time_ms and sections_scanned.

Overall flow (high level)
  1. Receive query and max_tokens.
  2. Search (keyword/semantic/hybrid) and rank candidate sections.
  3. Encode and count tokens with cl100k_base.
  4. Greedily select highest‑relevance sections until budget exhausted (use smart_truncate if needed).
  5. Return selected sections + metadata, total token usage, truncated flag, and suggestions.

If you want, I can show a small example trace (given a mock set of candidate sections) to illustrate how sections are picked and truncated.

Short answer
- Shared context collections are reusable team collections of coding standards, best practices, guidelines, and reference materials that are shared across projects so every project and developer automatically sees and follows the same rules.

What they contain
- Documents (SharedDocument) grouped into categories with priority:
  - MANDATORY (highest) — non‑negotiable rules (security, architecture)
  - BEST_PRACTICES — recommended patterns
  - GUIDELINES — helpful suggestions
  - REFERENCE — background/examples
- Each document has a priority value (0–100) to affect importance within its category.

How they work (workflow)
1. Team admin creates a Shared Context Collection (via dashboard or API).
2. Upload documents (rlm_upload_shared_document) and assign category + priority.
3. Link the collection to team projects in the dashboard. Linked projects automatically inherit the collection’s documents.
4. When you query project context:
   - Shared context is loaded first (and merged across linked collections).
   - Project‑specific docs are queried with the remaining token budget.
   - Token budgets for categories are allocated (examples in the docs show splits like MANDATORY 40% / BEST_PRACTICES 30% / GUIDELINES 20% / REFERENCE 10%; other examples show a shared-context vs project split such as 40% shared / 60% project or a configurable shared_context_budget_percent like 30%).
5. You can explicitly fetch merged team rules with rlm_shared_context (filter by categories, max_tokens, include_project_docs).
6. You can inject team standards into a session (rlm_inject) so they apply to subsequent queries and completions.
7. Templates from collections (rlm_list_templates, rlm_get_template) let you standardize prompts like code‑review checklists.

Key APIs / tools
- rlm_list_collections — view available collections
- rlm_upload_shared_document — add documents to a collection
- rlm_shared_context — fetch merged shared context
- rlm_context_query — project queries automatically include shared context first (configurable budget)
- rlm_list_templates, rlm_get_template — work with prompt templates

Other notes
- Team plan (or Enterprise) is required for team‑wide shared collections and auto‑enforcement features.
- Collections can be public or team‑scoped and include metadata (slug, scope, usageCount).
- Use priorities and focused documents (one topic per document) and update collections regularly to keep standards current.

If you want, I can summarize the exact API calls and example parameters for creating and using a shared collection from the docs.

Short answer
- Shared Context Collections are reusable team-level collections of documents (coding standards, best practices, guidelines, templates) that are linked to projects so every project/query can automatically inherit and apply the same team rules.

How they work (step‑by‑step)
1. Team admin creates a Shared Context Collection and uploads documents (e.g., via rlm_upload_shared_document). Documents are categorized (MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE) and given a priority.
2. Collections are linked to projects (via dashboard). Projects that link to a collection automatically inherit its documents.
3. At query time the shared context is merged with project docs (rlm_shared_context merges linked collections). Shared context is injected or queried first, then local project docs are searched with the remaining token budget.
4. Token budget is split by category and priority. Documents are returned in category priority order; documented token budgets are MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10% (and the system exposes parameters like max_tokens and shared_context_budget_percent to control allocation).
5. Developers and automation can call tools/APIs to access or use shared context:
   - rlm_shared_context to fetch merged team rules
   - rlm_context_query to run queries that automatically include shared context
   - rlm_list_templates / rlm_get_template for prompt templates stored in collections
   - rlm_upload_shared_document to add documents to a collection
   - rlm_inject to put shared rules into a session context so all subsequent queries follow them
6. Results are returned with shared context sections first, then project-specific sections. Multi-project queries can search across all team projects (rlm_multi_project_query).

Key behaviors & constraints
- Categories (and their relative importances) guide what gets included first — MANDATORY rules are non‑negotiable and always included.
- Priority (0–100) controls importance within a category.
- Team-wide shared context features require the Team (or Enterprise) plan.
- Collections can be public or team-scoped and include relations like documents and prompt templates.

Typical uses
- Enforcing coding/security standards automatically in every developer’s AI-assisted work
- Providing team code-review or feature templates
- Searching across projects for prior implementations (multi-project queries)
- Injecting team policy into sessions so generated code follows agreed rules

Best practices (from the docs)
- Start with MANDATORY rules, add BEST_PRACTICES gradually
- Keep documents focused (one topic per document)
- Use priority to indicate importance
- Update collections regularly and use templates for consistency

If you want, I can show the exact API calls and example payloads from the docs (upload, fetch, and inject) or walk through creating and linking a collection for your team.

Shared context collections are reusable sets of team-wide documents (coding standards, best practices, guidelines, templates, etc.) that are stored once and automatically included in queries across any projects that link to the collection. They let teams enforce consistent rules and patterns for every developer and every MCP query.

How they work (high level)
- Create a Shared Context Collection (team admin) and upload documents (rlm_upload_shared_document). Documents live in the collection and have a category (MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE) and a numeric priority (0–100) to tune importance within a category.
- Link the shared collection to projects via the dashboard; linked projects automatically inherit those documents.
- When you run project queries, the shared context is merged with project docs and injected into the query. Shared context is loaded first, then local project documents are queried with the remaining token budget.
- You can explicitly fetch merged shared context via rlm_shared_context (filters: categories, max_tokens, include_project_docs). rlm_shared_context returns the categorized documents, token budgeting info, and content you can inject into a session.
- Shared context can be injected into a session (rlm_inject) so that all subsequent queries apply the team rules.

Categories and token budgeting
- Documents are categorized and prioritized. Category priority order: MANDATORY (highest), BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE (lowest).
- Typical token budget distribution within shared context: MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10% (used to decide how much of each category is included when merging).
- The overall portion of a query’s token budget assigned to shared context can be configured (examples show a shared_context_budget_percent parameter, often illustrated at 30% or 40%); the remainder is used for project-specific documents.

Tools / APIs
- rlm_list_collections — list available collections (team/private/public).
- rlm_upload_shared_document — add documents to a collection.
- rlm_shared_context — fetch merged shared context from linked collections.
- rlm_list_templates / rlm_get_template — list and retrieve prompt templates stored in collections.
- rlm_context_query — project queries automatically include shared context first (with a configurable shared-context budget percent).

Other features & notes
- SharedContextCollection objects include metadata (name, slug, scope GLOBAL/TEAM/USER, isPublic, usageCount, relations to documents/templates).
- Multi-project search and team templates are supported (rlm_multi_project_query, template endpoints).
- Team plan (or Enterprise) is required for team-shared collections and auto-injection (not available on Free/Pro). Some enterprise-only features exist (custom categories, collection analytics).
- Best-practice recommendations: put non-negotiables in MANDATORY, keep documents focused, use priority to rank importance, update collections regularly, and use templates for consistency.

If you want, I can summarize the exact API calls and a sample workflow (steps and example parameters) for creating a collection, uploading standards, linking projects, and querying with the shared context.

Summary — how Snipara's OAuth Device Flow works

- Purpose: device flow (RFC 8628) lets users authenticate the MCP client without copying API keys. The CLI + browser complete the flow.

Steps (high level)
1. Start the flow from the CLI. The client calls POST /api/oauth/device/code with a body like:
   { "client_id": "snipara_cli" }
2. Server response includes:
   - device_code (opaque code for the client)
   - user_code (code the user types in)
   - verification_uri (URL the user visits)
   - verification_uri_complete (URL with the code prefilled)
   - expires_in (seconds until the device_code/user_code expire)
   - interval (suggested polling interval in seconds)
3. User opens the verification_uri (or verification_uri_complete) in a browser, enters the user_code (or follows the complete URL), and signs in/approves access.
4. The CLI uses the returned device_code and the provided interval/expires_in to wait/poll until the server issues an OAuth access token for the device.
5. On success the client stores the OAuth token (used like Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...) — the client typically stores tokens in ~/.snipara/tokens.json and will prefer that token when present.

How the token is used by MCP
- When making MCP requests the server accepts either:
  - OAuth: Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
  - API key: X-API-Key: rlm_...
- The MCP client checks for a stored OAuth token (~/.snipara/tokens.json) first, then falls back to the API key from environment variables.

Troubleshooting / alternatives
- If OAuth expires, re-run the device flow to re-authenticate.
- To bypass OAuth and force API-key auth you can:
  - Set SNIPARA_IGNORE_OAUTH=true in the MCP client environment (causes the client to skip ~/.snipara/tokens.json and use SNIPARA_API_KEY).
  - Or remove/backup ~/.snipara/tokens.json (rm or mv) so the client falls back to the API key.

If you want the exact POST /api/oauth/device/code request/response fields, see the example in the docs (client_id request; response fields listed above).

Short answer
- Snipara uses the OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Flow (RFC 8628). The user runs the MCP CLI, is shown a user code and verification URL, visits that URL in a browser, enters the code and signs in. The CLI then receives an OAuth access token and stores it locally for subsequent requests.

How it works (step‑by‑step, per the docs)
1. CLI requests a device code
   - The client POSTs { "client_id": "snipara_cli" } to POST /api/oauth/device/code.
   - The server responds with device_code, user_code, verification_uri, verification_uri_complete, expires_in (example: 900), and interval (example: 5).
2. User authenticates in browser
   - The CLI instructs the user to open verification_uri (or visit verification_uri_complete) and enter the user_code, then complete authentication in the browser.
3. CLI obtains the token
   - After the user completes authentication, the CLI receives an OAuth access token (not detailed in the provided docs) and stores it locally (the client checks ~/.snipara/tokens.json).
4. Use the token
   - Requests using OAuth should send: Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
   - If no valid OAuth token is found, the client falls back to API key auth.

Important details from the docs
- Authorization header formats:
  - OAuth: Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
  - API Key: X-API-Key: rlm_...
- Client priority: first check ~/.snipara/tokens.json for stored OAuth tokens, then fall back to API key.
- Example device-code response fields you’ll see: device_code, user_code, verification_uri, verification_uri_complete, expires_in, interval.
- If OAuth expires, re-run the device flow to re-authenticate.

Bypassing OAuth / troubleshooting
- Recommended: set SNIPARA_IGNORE_OAUTH=true in the MCP client env to force using the API key (example shown in the docs).
- Or remove/backup ~/.snipara/tokens.json to force API-key authentication.
- See docs/AUTHENTICATION.md for the full authentication documentation.

If you want any of these steps expanded (example CLI output, where tokens are stored, or how to configure SNIPARA_IGNORE_OAUTH), say which part and I’ll show the relevant details from the docs.

Short summary

OAuth Device Flow (RFC 8628) in Snipara lets users authenticate the MCP client from a browser (no API key copying). The CLI starts the flow, the user visits a URL and enters a code, and the client then receives and stores an OAuth access token for future requests.

Step-by-step (what happens)

1. The client starts the device authorization by POSTing to /api/oauth/device/code with:
   { "client_id": "snipara_cli" }

2. The server responds with fields such as:
   - device_code (server-side code)
   - user_code (what the user types in)
   - verification_uri (URL the user visits)
   - verification_uri_complete (URL with code included)
   - expires_in (seconds until the code expires)
   - interval (recommended polling interval)

   Example response (from docs):
   {
     "device_code":"abc123...",
     "user_code":"ABCD-1234",
     "verification_uri":"https://snipara.com/device",
     "verification_uri_complete":"https://snipara.com/device?code=ABCD-1234",
     "expires_in":900,
     "interval":5
   }

3. The CLI shows the user_code and URL (or opens verification_uri_complete). The user visits the URL in a browser, enters or confirms the code, and signs in/approves access.

4. After the user authorizes, the client gets an OAuth access token (used in requests as Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...). The client stores tokens locally (e.g., ~/.snipara/tokens.json).

Usage and server behavior

- When making requests, Snipara first checks for a stored OAuth token (~/.snipara/tokens.json) and uses it if present.
- If no valid OAuth token is available, the server falls back to API key authentication (X-API-Key: rlm_...).

Troubleshooting and alternatives

- If OAuth expires, re-run the device flow to re-authenticate.
- To force API-key authentication (skip OAuth) you can:
  - Set SNIPARA_IGNORE_OAUTH=true in the MCP client environment (client will skip ~/.snipara/tokens.json and use SNIPARA_API_KEY).
  - Or remove/backup ~/.snipara/tokens.json so the client falls back to the API key.

References in docs: RFC 8628 device flow mention, POST /api/oauth/device/code request/response schema, token storage (~/.snipara/tokens.json), and header formats (Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_... and X-API-Key: rlm_...).

From the provided documentation, the main Prisma database models in Snipara include:

Core models
- User
- Project
- TeamMember

Shared context / new models (defined in packages/database/prisma/schema.prisma)
- SharedContextCollection
- SharedDocument
- SharedMcpConfig
- PromptTemplate
- ProjectSharedContext

Other items called out in the docs
- TeamApiKey (team-scoped API keys)
- audit_logs (table exists in production)

Notes
- Prisma models use PascalCase singular (e.g., User) and are mapped to snake_case plural table names via @@map.  
- The source schema file is packages/database/prisma/schema.prisma.

From the provided docs, the main database models in Snipara are:

Core models
- User
- Project
- TeamMember
- Team (referenced in relations)

Shared-context / content models
- SharedContextCollection — collections of shared context/templates (scoped GLOBAL/TEAM/USER)
- SharedDocument — documents within a collection
- SharedMcpConfig — tool/configuration for a collection
- PromptTemplate — prompt templates within a collection
- ProjectSharedContext — junction table linking Project <-> SharedContextCollection (with per-link config)

Other notable models/tables

- audit_logs — audit log table (exists in production)

Notes: Prisma models use PascalCase (e.g., User, Project) and are mapped to snake_case plural table names in the DB; the schema in packages/database/prisma/schema.prisma is the source of truth.

Snipara uses a strict layered architecture (top → bottom):

- Routes (app/api/) — thin HTTP handlers (requireAuth, Zod validation, delegate to services)
- Services (lib/services/) — external API adapters (lazy init, retry)
- Repositories (lib/db/queries/) — centralized CRUD / permission-checked Prisma access
- Types & Utils — shared, pure code with no dependencies on other layers

Rules: lower layers cannot import from higher layers; all DB access goes through repository functions; route handlers must authenticate, validate, delegate, and return responses.

Snipara uses a strict layered (n‑tier) architecture. Layers (top → bottom):

- Routes (app/api/) — thin HTTP handlers
- Services (lib/services/) — external API adapters / business logic
- Repositories (lib/db/queries/) — centralized CRUD only
- Utils & Types (types/, lib/utils/) — shared, no dependencies

Rules: lower layers cannot import from higher layers; route handlers must authenticate, validate (Zod), delegate to services/queries, and return a response.

Snipara uses a strict layered architecture (layered/import-hierarchy) with thin route handlers. Layers, top → bottom:

- app/api/ (Routes — thin HTTP handlers)
- lib/jobs/ (Orchestrators / processors)
- lib/services/ (External API adapters / service layer)
- lib/db/queries/ (Repository layer — centralized CRUD + permission checks)
- types/, utils/ (Shared, pure helpers — no dependencies)

Key rules:
- Lower layers cannot import from higher layers.
- Route handlers must authenticate, validate (Zod), and delegate to services/queries (no direct Prisma calls).
- All DB access goes through lib/db/queries (repository pattern).

Short answer
- Snipara reduces your 500K-token codebase down to the small set of relevant text the LLM actually needs (example: ~5K tokens), so you only feed the LLM a few thousand tokens instead of hundreds of thousands — a ~90% context reduction (from the docs).
- That means your 128K-window LLM can easily consume the returned context without overflow, and you dramatically cut the number of tokens you pay the LLM provider for (Snipara itself does not run inference or charge LLM costs).

How it helps (mechanisms)
- Context optimization: Snipara searches (keyword/semantic/hybrid), ranks sections, and greedily selects content to fit a client-specified max_tokens budget (uses tiktoken/cl100k_base for accurate token counting).
- Token budgeting & smart truncation: it enforces max_tokens, truncates at sentence boundaries if needed, and returns token counts + metadata so you know exactly what you’re sending to the model.
- Session & shared context: session context can be persisted (up to a configured portion of the budget, documented as up to ~20%), and team “shared context” is injected first (defaults/examples show shared_context_budget_percent like 30%), so important rules are always included without bloating ad-hoc queries.
- Recursive tools for very large corpora: rlm_decompose + rlm_multi_query let you split a big question into sub-queries and assemble answers, enabling near-infinite-document workflows while staying within the LLM’s window.

What that means for costs
- You drastically reduce the number of tokens you send to the LLM (the docs cite reducing 500K tokens → ~5K relevant tokens, i.e., ~90% reduction). Since most LLM billing is per-token, that directly reduces your LLM inference bill.
- Snipara itself does not run inference (“No LLM Costs”): you only pay your LLM provider for the optimized token payloads Snipara returns.
- Additional savings come from session/context persistence and shared collections (you avoid repeatedly sending the same long documents each call), and from decomposition (smaller targeted queries rather than re-sending huge contexts).

If you want a concrete cost estimate
- Not found in the provided documentation — the docs show % reductions and workflow benefits but do not provide LLM pricing or dollar-savings examples. To estimate dollars saved you’d multiply the reduced token count per request (e.g., ~5K instead of 500K) by your LLM provider’s per-token rate and factor in expected request volume.

Short answer
- Snipara reduces a huge codebase (example: 500K tokens) to a small, relevant context (the docs show ~5K tokens) so you only send what matters to your LLM. That compressed context easily fits inside a 128K model window and preserves session/shared context and metadata.
- Cost impact: you pay Snipara’s plan fee (free → $0, Pro → $19/mo, Team → $49/mo, Enterprise → $499/mo) but you avoid paying LLM inference on the full 500K tokens — you only pay your LLM for the much smaller optimized context.

How Snipara helps (mechanics)
- Context reduction: the service finds and ranks relevant document sections and returns an optimized set that fits your token budget (example claim: 500K → ~5K relevant tokens).
- Token budgeting & smart truncation: you set max_tokens (e.g., to fit under 128K); Snipara counts tokens with tiktoken, greedily selects highest-relevance sections, and smart-truncates at sentence boundaries to fit the budget.
- Shared/team context: shared rules/docs can be included first with a configurable budget percent so team standards still travel with queries.
- Session persistence: session context can be included across compaction (up to configured budget share).
- Recursive tools for very large queries: rlm_decompose + rlm_multi_query let you split a big question into sub-queries so the LLM plus Snipara can cover 500K+ docs in multiple budgeted calls while each call fits the 128K (or smaller) window.
- No extra inference fees from Snipara: Snipara doesn’t run LLM inference — you use your own LLM and pay that provider only for the (much smaller) context you send.

How to reason about cost savings (how to estimate)
- Use the token reduction directly: Savings per query ≈ (tokens_before - tokens_after) × your_LLM_cost_per_token.
  - Example inputs you already have: tokens_before = 500,000; tokens_after ≈ 5,000 (per the docs).
- Also save on engineering time and latency (smaller prompts are faster, fewer retries, fewer multi-turn retrievals).
- If you need to cover the whole codebase, the recursive workflow lets you distribute work into multiple budgeted queries rather than sending or re-requesting huge slices repeatedly — that reduces wasted repeated token usage.

Practical next steps
1. Configure rlm_context_query with max_tokens set to fit well under your 128K window (and include_shared_context/shared_context_budget_percent if you want team docs included first).
2. For very large or complex questions, use rlm_decompose → rlm_multi_query to process sub-queries in budgeted chunks.
3. Estimate dollar savings by plugging your LLM per-token price into the simple formula above.

If you want, tell me your LLM per-token price (or provider) and how many queries/month you expect and I can show a worked example estimate using the documented token reduction.

Short answer
- Use rlm_context_query for normal documentation/code lookups and any single-step question.
- Use rlm_decompose when the question is complex, large-scale, or must be broken into ordered sub-queries (near-infinite context / multi-step orchestration).

When to pick which (details)

- rlm_context_query
  - Primary/first choice for documentation Q&A, code lookup, and simple retrievals.
  - Fast, cheaper, and more accurate for single-step queries.
  - Example use: “Where is auth handled?” or “How does the login flow work?” (small-to-moderate scope).

- rlm_decompose
  - Use for complex research that exceeds a single-query context window or requires a planned sequence of sub-questions.
  - Returns sub_queries, dependencies, suggested_sequence, and token estimates so you can inspect/modify the plan before execution (works with rlm_multi_query, etc.).
  - Good for “explain the complete authentication system” across many files, or any task needing recursive decomposition and orchestration.
  - Note: rlm_decompose is a Power User / Pro+ tool in the docs.

Practical pattern
1. Try rlm_context_query first for straightforward documentation/code questions.
2. If the answer is incomplete or the question is large/complex → run rlm_decompose to get sub-queries and a plan, then execute those (e.g., with rlm_multi_query) and synthesize the results.

This follows the docs’ recommendation to prefer direct Snipara MCP tools for simple queries and use the recursive/decomposition tools when you need near‑infinite context or multi-step orchestration.

Short answer
- Use rlm_context_query for normal documentation/code Q&A and single-step lookups.
- Use rlm_decompose when the question is complex, exceeds a single context window, or you want an explicit sub‑query + execution plan before running searches.

Why / when to pick each

- rlm_context_query
  - Best first choice for documentation queries, code lookups, and any question with a clear, single‑step answer.
  - Faster, cheaper, and more accurate for routine retrievals (recommended to try this first).
- rlm_decompose
  - Use for complex, multi-part investigations (large systems, audits, "explain the complete X" type questions).
  - Returns sub‑queries, dependencies, estimated tokens, and a suggested sequence so you can inspect/modify the plan before executing (used with rlm_multi_query/rlm_plan to handle very large/recursive context).
  - Intended for power‑user / Pro+ workflows where orchestration overhead is justified.

Example
- Simple: "Where is auth handled?" → rlm_context_query.
- Complex: "Explain the complete authentication system across login, token generation, session storage, and revocation" → rlm_decompose (get sub‑queries + plan), then run the subqueries (e.g., with rlm_multi_query) and synthesize results.

Also remember: rlm_decompose adds orchestration overhead, so only use it when the complexity/context needs justify it.

Use rlm_context_query for normal documentation/code lookups and rlm_decompose when the question is complex and needs planned, multi-step retrieval.

When to use rlm_context_query
- Single-step documentation Q&A (e.g., "Where is auth handled?", "How does X feature work?")
- Code lookup or pattern search
- Simple retrievals that fit in one context fetch
- Faster, cheaper, and more accurate for straightforward queries (it's the primary tool)

When to use rlm_decompose
- The question is complex or covers many documents (e.g., “Explain the complete authentication system”)
- You need an explicit breakdown into sub-queries, priorities, dependencies, and token budgets
- You want to inspect/modify the sub-queries or execution plan before running them
- You plan to run sub-queries in parallel (use rlm_multi_query afterward) to handle very large/near‑infinite context

Recommended workflow
1. Try rlm_context_query first for simple/medium queries.
2. If the answer is incomplete or the task is multi-part, call rlm_decompose to get sub-queries + execution plan.
3. Execute the sub-queries (rlm_multi_query or sequential calls) and synthesize the final answer.

Note: rlm_decompose (and rlm_multi_query) are power-user / Pro+ tools intended for recursive, large-context workflows.

Short answer
- Use keyword for exact term / pattern matching (free).
- Use semantic for conceptual, “how does X work?” or architecture questions (Pro+).
- Use hybrid when you want the best overall results for general/broad documentation or best-practices searches (Pro+).

Details
- keyword (Free): exact-term matching. Best for “where is this function?”, error-code lookups, API endpoint discovery, or any query where a specific token or filename will find the answer quickly.
- semantic (Pro+): embedding similarity / conceptual matching. Best for “How does X work?”, architecture understanding, and other questions that require understanding intent or paraphrases rather than exact words.
- hybrid (Pro+): combines keyword + semantic and gives the best overall results for general documentation searches, best-practice lookups, and broad discovery tasks.

Quick guidance
- If you know a filename/function name or need an exact match → keyword.
- If you need explanation, architecture, or conceptual context → semantic.
- If you want the strongest, most robust search across varied docs → hybrid.

Use the mode that matches how specific or conceptual your query is.

Modes (short):
- keyword — basic term matching (Free)
- semantic — embedding similarity, for conceptual/architectural queries (Pro+)
- hybrid — combined (best results) (Pro+)

Recommended mapping (typical queries)
- Looking for a specific function / API endpoint discovery / error code lookup → keyword  
  Rationale: exact-term matches are fast and precise.
- "How does X work?" / architecture understanding → semantic  
  Rationale: concept-level similarity finds explanatory docs across files.
- General documentation search / best practices → hybrid  
  Rationale: combines exact matches with semantic relevance for broader coverage.

Note: semantic and hybrid require Pro+ plan; keyword is available on Free.

Use the mode that matches the query type and desired precision. Short summary:

- keyword — basic term matching. Use when you know exact names/terms (functions, endpoints, error codes). Plan: Free.
- semantic — embedding similarity. Use for conceptual, “how does X work?” or architecture questions where wording varies. Plan: Pro+.
- hybrid — combined keyword + semantic (best of both). Use for broad documentation searches and best-practices queries where you want both coverage and precision. Plan: Pro+.

Quick guidance (from the docs):
- Looking for a specific function → keyword
- “How does X work?” questions → semantic
- General documentation search → hybrid
- Error code lookup → keyword
- Architecture understanding → semantic
- API endpoint discovery → keyword
- Best practices search → hybrid

If you have Pro+ access, prefer semantic or hybrid for conceptual/architecture/best-practices queries; use keyword for fast, exact-term lookups (and when on the Free plan).

Available authentication options (from the provided docs)
- Project API Key
  - Header: X-API-Key: rlm_...
  - Key format prefix: rlm_
  - Lookup: server hashes the key with SHA-256 and compares to stored hash (project keys tried first, then team keys)
  - Use case: single project access
- Team API Key
  - Header: X-API-Key: rlm_...
  - Same storage/lookup as project keys
  - Use case: access across all projects in a team
- OAuth (Device Flow) tokens
  - Header: Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
  - Token format prefix: snipara_at_
  - Flow: RFC 8628 device flow (CLI shows code/URL; user authorizes in browser)
  - Tokens (access and refresh) are stored as hashes (OAuthToken), include tokenPrefix (first 12 chars) and scope metadata (e.g., "mcp:read mcp:write")
  - Use case: device/CLI authentication without copying API keys; supports scope-based access control

Where each is accepted
- MCP server endpoints accept either OAuth Bearer tokens or X-API-Key.
- Inject API endpoints require X-API-Key (API key only).

How they differ in security (summary)
- Storage security on server
  - API keys: stored as SHA-256 hashes in the DB.
  - OAuth tokens: stored as hashed tokens (access and refresh) in the DB.
- Scope & least-privilege
  - OAuth tokens include explicit scopes (e.g., mcp:read, mcp:write) so you can limit capabilities.
  - API keys are project- or team-scoped (project key vs team key) but do not use OAuth scopes.
- Lifespan & revocation
  - OAuth access tokens have expirations and refresh tokens; expired/revoked OAuth tokens cause a 401 and require re-authentication. There is a DELETE /api/settings/tokens/[tokenId] endpoint to revoke tokens.
  - API keys are long-lived until rotated or deleted in the dashboard (the troubleshooting guidance for an invalid API key is to generate a new one).
- Client-side handling & attack surface
  - Device flow (OAuth) avoids copying API keys into clients by using a browser-based grant, reducing risk of accidental leakage.
  - API keys are static credentials that, if exposed, grant immediate access until rotated. The system shows only the first 8 chars in UI/logs to limit exposure; logs use prefix-only for lookup.
  - OAuth tokens are stored locally (e.g., CLI may keep tokens at ~/.snipara/tokens.json); that storage can be removed to force API-key usage.
- Authentication priority and compatibility
  - MCP server checks for a stored OAuth token first (e.g., local tokens.json) and falls back to API key from environment.
  - Some endpoints (Inject API) only accept API keys, so OAuth cannot be used everywhere.

Practical notes
- To force API-key auth from a CLI that stores OAuth tokens, remove or move ~/.snipara/tokens.json.
- For expired OAuth tokens, re-authenticate via device flow or dashboard.
- If you need limited-scoped access with automatic expiry and refresh, prefer OAuth device-flow tokens. For simple project- or team-level programmatic access where device flow is not available, use API keys.

Available authentication options (from the provided docs)
1. Project API key
   - Header: X-API-Key: rlm_...
   - Intended use: single-project access
   - Implementation notes: key value has rlm_ prefix and is SHA‑256 hashed in the DB; lookup tries project keys first then falls back to team keys; server returns user_id, access_level and key metadata.
2. Team API key
   - Header: X-API-Key: rlm_...
   - Intended use: access all projects in a team
   - Implementation notes: same key format and DB hashing as project keys, but different scope of access (team-wide).
3. OAuth (Device Flow) tokens
   - Header: Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
   - Intended use: MCP/device-flow authentication (RFC 8628) so users can authenticate via browser without copying API keys.
   - Implementation notes: access and refresh tokens have snipara_at_ prefix, are stored hashed in the database, include scope strings (e.g., "mcp:read mcp:write"), and carry expiry timestamps. Device flow endpoints are provided for polling/issuance and there are APIs to list and revoke tokens.

How they differ in security (summary from docs)
- Secret storage and handling
  - Both API keys and OAuth tokens are stored hashed server‑side (API keys hashed with SHA‑256; OAuth access/refresh tokens stored as hashes). Key/token values are not exposed in full in UI/logs (only prefixes/first chars shown).
- Granularity and scopes
  - OAuth tokens support scopes (explicit scope strings and server‑side enforcement), enabling finer-grained permission control (e.g., read vs write).
  - API keys provide project‑ or team‑level access; the system returns an access_level but scopes are not described in the docs the same way as OAuth scopes.
- Lifetimes and revocation
  - OAuth tokens have explicit expiry fields (accessExpiresAt, refreshExpiresAt) and can be revoked via settings APIs (GET /api/settings/tokens and DELETE /api/settings/tokens/[tokenId]).
  - The docs do not describe API key expirations; API keys are longer‑lived credentials (regeneration is done via the dashboard per troubleshooting guidance).
- Attack surface / operational security
  - OAuth device flow avoids copying API keys into places where they could be leaked (users authenticate in a browser), reducing credential exposure during setup.
  - API keys are static secrets that must be protected; they are sent in X-API-Key headers and are suitable for server-to-server calls (Inject API and MCP endpoints accept them).
- Endpoint support and precedence
  - MCP endpoints accept either Authorization: Bearer (OAuth) or X-API-Key; Inject API endpoints accept X-API-Key only.
  - Client behavior: the client checks for stored OAuth tokens (~/.snipara/tokens.json) first and falls back to API key from environment variables. Removing the token file forces use of API key (docs show rm ~/.snipara/tokens.json or mv to backup).

Practical takeaway
- Use OAuth device flow when you want scoped, time‑limited credentials and browser-based auth (less risk of copying/storing long‑lived secrets).
- Use project or team API keys for server-to-server integrations or when scope/short‑lived tokens are not required, but protect them carefully (they are static secrets).

Available authentication options (from the provided docs)
- Project API key
  - Header: X-API-Key: rlm_...
  - Format: keys start with rlm_ and are SHA‑256 hashed in the database
  - Lookup: server hashes the presented key and compares against ApiKey table (project key checked first, then team key)
  - Use case: single-project access
- Team API key
  - Header: X-API-Key: rlm_...
  - Same format/lookup as project key
  - Use case: access across all projects in a team (broader scope than a project key)
- OAuth (Device Flow)
  - Header: Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
  - Device flow (RFC 8628): CLI opens a browser and user authenticates with a code; tokens returned include access_token and refresh_token
  - Token model: tokens stored as hashes, include tokenPrefix, scopes, accessExpiresAt and optional refreshExpiresAt; refresh tokens supported
  - Endpoints: MCP server accepts either OAuth bearer or X-API-Key; the Inject API endpoint accepts X-API-Key

How they differ in security (from the provided docs)
- Storage and verification
  - API keys: hashed (SHA‑256) in DB; server compares hashes on incoming keys.
  - OAuth tokens: stored as hashes and validated by DB lookup plus expiry check.
- Scope and access granularity
  - OAuth tokens explicitly carry scopes (e.g., "mcp:read mcp:write") and the system enforces read/write scopes.
  - API keys distinguish project vs team keys (team keys grant broader access across projects).
- Lifetimes and revocation
  - OAuth tokens have explicit expiry fields (accessExpiresAt, refreshExpiresAt) and support refresh tokens; expired or revoked OAuth tokens produce a 401 and require re‑authentication. (Example response shows expires_in: 2592000.)
  - The provided docs do not document expiry for API keys; token revocation endpoints are available for OAuth tokens (GET /api/settings/tokens, DELETE /api/settings/tokens/[tokenId]).
- Exposure risk and UX
  - Device flow OAuth avoids copying or distributing long‑lived API keys (reduces risk of exposing keys when authenticating from a CLI).
  - API keys are presented directly to the client (X-API-Key) and therefore must be kept secret; UI/logs only show a truncated prefix.
- Endpoint support / precedence
  - MCP server accepts both auth types and prefers a stored OAuth token (~/.snipara/tokens.json) before falling back to API key environment variables. Removing that tokens file forces use of API keys.
  - The Inject API (REST automation inject) uses X-API-Key, so OAuth bearer tokens are not supported there per the docs.

If you want a recommendation or a concise security tradeoff (short-lived, scoping, revocability vs simplicity), tell me which use-case you have and I’ll summarize.

Short answer
- Token budget is split between shared context documents and project documents, then shared documents are further split by category (MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE). Which percentages apply depends on the API/flow you call and any configurable parameters.

How it works (details from the docs/code)
1. Two-phase split (shared vs project)
   - When you run a combined query (e.g., rlm_context_query), the system first includes shared context and then fills the remaining budget with project docs. There is an explicit parameter you can pass: shared_context_budget_percent (example JSON shows 30).
   - The token-budgeting code also uses a default allocation that splits overall budget between project docs and shared categories (see DEFAULT_ALLOCATION in token-budget.ts).

2. Shared-only distribution (rlm_shared_context)
   - When fetching merged shared collections by themselves (rlm_shared_context), the shared collection’s internal category split is documented as:
     - MANDATORY: 40%
     - BEST_PRACTICES: 30%
     - GUIDELINES: 20%
     - REFERENCE: 10%
   - Example response in the docs shows a budget_allocation with those percentages.

3. Combined allocation defaults (implementation-level)
   - The token-budget.ts DEFAULT_ALLOCATION used by allocateTokenBudget is:
     - project: 40%
     - MANDATORY: 25%
     - BEST_PRACTICES: 20%
     - GUIDELINES: 10%
     - REFERENCE: 5%
   - Those percentages sum to 100% and are used to compute per-category budgets as floor(totalBudget * allocation).

4. How documents are chosen within each budget
   - Docs are grouped (project vs shared-by-category).
   - Within each group, docs are sorted by relevanceScore (if present) and then selected in order until that group’s token budget is exhausted. Each doc is marked allocated=true if it fits, otherwise allocated=false with reason "exceeded budget".
   - There are hard limits applied too (e.g., MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION), and enforceCollectionLimits will truncate collections that exceed those limits.

What you can configure / control
- Use rlm_context_query include_shared_context and shared_context_budget_percent to control how much of the total budget goes to shared context vs project docs.
- There is a DEFAULT_ALLOCATION in code, but allocateTokenBudget accepts a customAllocation to override allocations programmatically.

Relevant functions / params in the docs
- rlm_shared_context(categories, max_tokens)
- rlm_context_query(query, max_tokens, include_shared_context, shared_context_budget_percent)
- allocateTokenBudget(docs, totalBudget, query, customAllocation)
- enforceCollectionLimits(...) and LIMITS constants

If you want a concrete example for your project setup, tell me which API/flow you’ll call (rlm_shared_context alone vs rlm_context_query combining project docs) and the total token budget you plan to use, and I’ll walk through the exact allocation step-by-step.

Short answer
- The token budget is split between project docs and shared context, and the shared context is further split by category (MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE). Project docs are allocated first, then shared docs are allocated category-by-category until each category’s budget is exhausted.

How it works (details from the docs/code)
1. Two layers of allocation
   - Project scope vs shared context: project documents get a dedicated portion of the total token budget and are allocated first.
   - Shared context is split into categories (MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE) and each category receives a portion of the budget.

2. Default/nominal allocations shown in the docs
   - High-level documentation and examples use a shared-context category split of:
     - MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10% (when returning only shared context).
   - MCP query examples also show a shared_context_budget_percent parameter (e.g., 30% for shared, 70% for project).
   - The token-budget implementation (apps/web/lib/services/token-budget.ts) defines DEFAULT_ALLOCATION as:
     - project: 40%,
     - MANDATORY: 25%,
     - BEST_PRACTICES: 20%,
     - GUIDELINES: 10%,
     - REFERENCE: 5%.
   - Note: the code-based DEFAULT_ALLOCATION may differ from the high-level documentation/examples. The allocation is configurable (see next points).

3. Allocation algorithm (from token-budget.ts)
   - Group documents by source (project vs shared) and by shared category.
   - Compute a budget for projectDocs: Math.floor(totalBudget * allocation.project) and allocate project docs first.
   - For each shared category, compute categoryBudget = Math.floor(totalBudget * allocation[category]) and allocate within that budget.
   - Within each group, docs are sorted by relevanceScore (if present) and then included until the token sum would exceed the group budget; included docs are marked allocated=true, others allocated=false with reason "exceeded budget".
   - The allocation mapping can be overridden by passing a customAllocation to allocateTokenBudget.

4. Configuration hooks / parameters
   - rlm_context_query supports include_shared_context and shared_context_budget_percent to control how much of the query budget goes to shared context vs project docs.
   - rlm_shared_context supports max_tokens and category filtering (e.g., only MANDATORY and BEST_PRACTICES).

5. Hard limits
   - There are collection-level limits enforced (e.g., MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION). If a collection exceeds the hard token limit, enforceCollectionLimits truncates documents from that collection to fit the limit.

Practical takeaway
- Expect shared context to be included early in queries and the budget to be split between project and shared docs. The platform provides default splits (documented and code defaults differ), but allocations are configurable via parameters or by passing custom allocation to the budget routine. Allocation within each bucket is relevance-driven and stops when the bucket’s token quota is filled.

When a user exceeds their monthly query limit the API returns an error (HTTP 429 Too Many Requests) with rate-limit details. Example response shown in the docs:

{
  "success": false,
  "error": "Monthly query limit exceeded",
  "usage": {
    "current": 101,
    "max": 100,
    "exceeded": true,
    "reset_at": "2026-02-01T00:00:00Z"
  }
}

Key details returned:
- `usage.current`: current usage this billing period  
- `usage.max`: maximum allowed (or -1 for unlimited)  
- `usage.exceeded`: true when limit reached  
- `usage.reset_at`: ISO timestamp when the monthly limit resets

The docs also show related error formats such as an INSufficient_CREDITS error for query-credit issues and a RATE_LIMIT_EXCEEDED response with a message like "Try again in 60 seconds."

Not found in a single explicit sentence, but the behavior is defined by the provided code and UI guidance:

- The context query handler will return an empty list of sections (no content selected) and total_tokens = 0 (nothing returned).
- The response fields still appear: "sections": [], "total_tokens": 0, "truncated": false, and "session_context" will be included if available. The "suggestions" field is still generated (via _generate_suggestions).
- UI guidance (Empty States) says to present a helpful empty-state: a clear message, generous padding (py-8) and action buttons (e.g., upload docs, try a different query).

The docs don’t spell out a specific rlm_context_query return value for “no results,” but they do give guidance for empty states:

- Show a helpful message when there’s no data (e.g., “No results” / “No tasks available”).
- Use padding (py-8) rather than fixed heights for the UI.
- Include action buttons (e.g., retry, upload docs, or links to relevant pages).

Note: the context_query implementation shown does not include an explicit code path for a zero-results response, so the exact tool-level behavior/JSON when no documents are found is not specified in the provided documentation.

If no relevant documents are found, the context-query handler simply returns no sections and no tokens — i.e. an empty sections list and total_tokens = 0. The response still includes other metadata (truncated will be false, session_context if available, and any suggestions generated). The docs also recommend the UI surface a helpful empty state (a message, padding, and action buttons) when there’s no data.
